worth_it_explanation: Reporting accuracy decides whether the revenue and fill numbers a publisher reads in Equativ match the money that actually arrives and match what partner systems report for the same inventory. A report that counts impressions a different way, or runs on a different timezone than the systems it is compared against, can send a publisher chasing a discrepancy that is really a configuration difference, or hide one that is real. The check reads the reconciliation a publisher already does and the report settings already in place, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete setting or process the publisher can correct.
intro: Reporting accuracy on Equativ is about whether the platform's revenue and fill numbers can be trusted as a basis for decisions. Two questions sit underneath that trust. The first is whether reported revenue and fill reconcile against the payments and remittances a publisher actually receives, so the numbers in the platform match the money in the bank. The second is whether report dimensions and timezones are configured so the numbers line up with the partner systems, billing exports, and analytics tools a publisher compares them against. These checks confirm that the reported figures are reconciled to real settlements and that they are built to be comparable, rather than accepted at face value.

audit_checks.title: Reported revenue and fill are not reconciled against actual payments
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Platform-reported revenue is an estimate of what an auction cleared, and the money a publisher is actually paid can differ once invalid traffic deductions, currency conversion, partner-side discrepancies, and platform fees are applied. A publisher who never reconciles the reported figure against the remittance cannot tell whether the gap is normal settlement behavior or a real shortfall, so a missing payment or a worsening discrepancy can go unnoticed across pay cycles.

educate.base:
Revenue a sell-side platform reports is the platform's record of what impressions cleared for in its own auctions. The amount a publisher is actually paid is a separate number that arrives later, after a chain of adjustments has been applied. Invalid traffic the platform or a buyer flags after the fact gets deducted. Bids that cleared in one currency get converted to the currency the publisher is paid in. A demand partner's own count of impressions or revenue may differ from the platform's count for the same inventory, and the settlement follows the partner's books. Platform fees come out before the remittance. Each adjustment is a legitimate part of how the money settles, and each one opens a gap between the reported figure and the deposit.

The gap itself is expected. The problem is not reconciling it. When a publisher reads reported revenue in Equativ and never compares it against the payment that lands for the same period, there is no baseline for what a normal gap looks like, so there is no way to tell a routine settlement difference from a real shortfall. A demand partner that stops paying for a slice of inventory, a fee change that was never communicated, or a deduction that grows month over month all hide inside an unreconciled number.

Currency conversion deserves particular attention because it produces confusion rather than error. Header bidding is a first-price auction, so the clearing price is the bid itself, and when bids arrive in several currencies they are converted to the publisher's reporting currency before the totals are built. Prebid's own documentation describes this conversion for Prebid-compatible setups, and Equativ is Prebid-compatible, so a publisher reading a converted total should know which currency the report is denominated in and at what rate since a rate difference alone can move the reported figure away from the remittance.

Reconciliation does not require special tooling. It requires a repeatable comparison. For each pay period, a publisher reads the revenue and fill Equativ reports for the inventory in question, lines it up against the remittance statement for the same period and the same inventory, and records the difference along with the reason for it. Over a few cycles, that produces a known, explainable gap, and any movement away from that known gap becomes a signal worth investigating.

A few signs point to revenue and fill that are not reconciled:
  • No record exists comparing Equativ's reported revenue for a period against the payment received for that period
  • The reported currency and the conversion rate behind a converted total are not documented, so a currency difference cannot be ruled out as the cause of a gap
  • Demand-partner discrepancies are noticed only when a payment is visibly short, rather than tracked period over period
You may want to confirm that reported revenue and fill are reconciled against actual remittances on a regular cycle. One approach is to build a per-period comparison of reported revenue against the payment statement for the same inventory, record the gap and its reasons, and treat any drift from that known gap as a flag.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, revenue often flows from several demand partners and programmatic deals at once, and each settles on its own statement and schedule. You may want to reconcile reported revenue against the remittance partner by partner rather than against a single blended deposit, so a shortfall from one partner is not masked by the others paying as expected.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, ad revenue is usually one income stream alongside product sales, so it can receive less reconciliation attention than the storefront. You may want to give the ad-revenue remittance the same period-over-period comparison the rest of the business gets since an unreconciled ad figure is easy to overlook when it is the smaller line.

investigate.intro: Review whether a repeatable comparison exists between Equativ's reported revenue and fill and the payments actually received for the same periods and the same inventory, and whether the reporting currency and conversion rates are documented.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Open the platform's reporting and pull revenue, impressions, and fill rate for a closed pay period, broken out by the demand partner or inventory the publisher settles against.
Record the reporting currency and, where bids cleared in other currencies, the conversion rate applied to reach the reported total.
Save the period's figures so they can be lined up against the remittance for the same period and inventory.

remittance_records.label: Payment and remittance statements
steps: Gather the remittance or payment statement that covers the same period and the same inventory as the reporting pull.

Line up the reported revenue against the amount paid, and note each adjustment that explains a difference, e.g., invalid traffic deductions, currency conversion, partner-side discrepancies, or platform fees.

Ask the publisher whether this comparison is run every pay period and where the results are kept, and record whether a known, explained gap exists or whether the reported figure is accepted without comparison.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for at least one closed pay period, the revenue and fill Equativ reported and the payment actually received for the same inventory, along with the reasons for any difference and the reporting currency and conversion rate. A setup where a repeatable reconciliation exists and produces a known, explained gap passes. A setup where no comparison between reported revenue and remittance is run, or where the gap cannot be explained, is a fail recorded with the period and the inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the reported revenue and fill and the matching remittance for a closed period above, along with the reasons for any gap, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not reconcile the revenue and fill reported in Equativ against the payments actually received for the same inventory. Reported revenue is the platform's record of what auctions cleared for, and the remittance arrives after invalid traffic deductions, currency conversion, partner-side discrepancies, and fees are applied, so a gap is expected. Without a regular comparison there is no baseline for a normal gap, which means a real shortfall or a growing deduction can pass unnoticed. {{voice_recommend}} building a per-period comparison of reported revenue against the remittance for the same inventory, recording the gap and the reasons for it, and treating any drift from that known gap as a flag. Documenting the reporting currency and the conversion rate behind any converted total rules out a currency difference as the cause of a gap.

audit_checks.title: Report dimensions and timezone are not aligned for comparison across systems
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When an Equativ report runs on a different timezone, date boundary, or impression-counting basis than the partner system, billing export, or analytics tool it is compared against, the same inventory produces two numbers that will not match even when both are correct. A publisher who treats that built-in mismatch as a data problem spends effort chasing a discrepancy that configuration created, and may miss a real one hiding underneath the noise.

educate.base:
Two reports describing the same inventory will agree only when they are built on the same definitions. Timezone sets where each day begins and ends, so a report on the publisher's local time and a partner report on Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) split impressions into days at different moments, and a daily comparison between them will never tie out even when both are accurate. The same effect appears at the boundary of a month, where a few hours of difference shift revenue from one billing period into the next.

Dimensions create the second source of mismatch. A report grouped by ad unit, by placement, by demand channel, or by buyer slices the same totals along different lines, and comparing a report built one way against a report built another way puts numbers side by side that were never meant to match. Counting basis is the third. Impression measurement guidelines from the Media Rating Council and IAB Tech Lab moved the industry to a count-on-begin-to-render standard, which counts an impression later in delivery than older served-impression methods, so a system on one basis will report fewer impressions than a system on an earlier basis for the same traffic. The reconciliation work those guidelines describe exists precisely because accredited systems can count the same impressions differently.

None of these differences is an error on its own. Each becomes a problem when a publisher compares two reports without first aligning them, then reads the built-in gap as a data-quality failure. The effort goes into explaining a discrepancy that the report settings created, and a genuine discrepancy can sit unnoticed inside the noise.

The fix is to make the comparison fair before drawing conclusions from it. That means knowing the timezone each report runs on and converting to a common one, matching the date and month boundaries, grouping both reports by the same dimensions, and knowing the impression-counting basis on each side. Equativ's own report configuration controls the timezone and dimensions on its side, and those settings deserve to be recorded so the comparison rests on known definitions rather than assumptions.

A few signs point to dimensions and timezone that are not aligned:
  • An Equativ report and the system it is compared against run on different timezones, with no conversion to a common one before the numbers are read side by side
  • The two reports are grouped by different dimensions, e.g., ad unit on one side and buyer on the other, yet compared as though they describe the same slice
  • The impression-counting basis on each side is unknown, so a difference in counts cannot be separated from a real discrepancy
You may want to confirm that reports are aligned on timezone, date boundaries, dimensions, and counting basis before any cross-system comparison. One approach is to record those four settings for each report involved and convert them to a common basis before reading the numbers against each other.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, traffic and revenue often peak at predictable hours, so a timezone offset between two reports can move a meaningful block of impressions across the day boundary and exaggerate a daily mismatch. You may want to compare on a common timezone and on weekly or monthly totals where the offset matters less, then narrow to daily comparisons only after the reports are aligned.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, ad reporting is frequently lined up against an analytics or order-management tool that runs on the storefront's timezone, which may differ from the ad platform's. You may want to set both the ad report and the comparison tool to the same timezone and date boundaries so ad revenue lines up with the commercial reporting it sits beside.

learn_more.source_title: MRC, joined by IAB Tech Lab and MMA, releases revised ad measurement guidelines

investigate.intro: Review the timezone, date boundaries, dimensions, and impression-counting basis of the Equativ report and of each system it is compared against, and check whether they are aligned to a common basis before the numbers are read side by side.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Open a report the publisher uses for cross-system comparison and record its timezone setting, its date and month boundaries, and the dimensions it is grouped by.
Note the impression-counting basis the platform applies, e.g., a count-on-begin-to-render measured impression versus a served count, so it can be matched against the other system.
Save these settings so the report can be aligned to a common basis with the system it is compared against.

comparison_system.label: Comparison system reporting
steps: For each partner system, billing export, or analytics tool the publisher compares against, record its timezone, date boundaries, dimensions, and impression-counting basis.
Line up the settings against the Equativ report and note every place they differ, e.g., a UTC versus local-time timezone, a different month boundary, or a different counting basis.
Ask the publisher whether reports are converted to a common basis before comparison, and record whether the cross-system comparison rests on aligned definitions or on reports built differently.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the timezone, date boundaries, dimensions, and impression-counting basis for the Equativ report and for each system it is compared against. A setup where those four settings are known and converted to a common basis before comparison passes. A setup where reports are compared without aligning timezone, boundaries, dimensions, or counting basis is a fail recorded with the specific settings that differ and the comparison they affect.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the timezone, date boundaries, dimensions, and counting basis for each report being compared above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} compares its Equativ reports against other systems without first aligning timezone, date boundaries, dimensions, and impression-counting basis. The same inventory produces two numbers that will not match when one report runs on local time and another on Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), when the two are grouped by different dimensions, or when each counts impressions on a different basis, even though both can be correct. Reading that built-in gap as a data problem sends effort toward a discrepancy that configuration created and can hide a real one underneath. {{voice_recommend}} recording those four settings for every report involved and converting them to a common basis before reading the numbers against each other, so a remaining difference points to a genuine discrepancy rather than a definition mismatch.
